submitted.idea: User guide to make your AI generated images look more realistic and not more AI-slop - cite the ChatGPT and Midjourney methods, and give a list of prompts for both services that will help the user make their images look more real and lifelike, not AI-slop
submitted.editorial_notes: The article should cover the basics for image prompting, what to do and not do when it comes to trying to create and image that doesn't look like obvious AI. It should cover ChatGPT and Midjourney and have a breakdown and section for both, as well as, explain how the prompts should vary between services to get the best results

brief.target_reader: Someone who has tried AI image generation once or twice and keeps getting results that look obviously fake or 'AI-ish' — glossy skin, weird hands, too-perfect lighting, that unmistakable synthetic sheen. They've heard of ChatGPT image gen and Midjourney but aren't sure how to use either well. Not a designer or photographer, just a curious creator who wants better results today.
brief.reader_outcome: Feel confident writing prompts for both ChatGPT and Midjourney that push results toward realistic, grounded images instead of the generic AI-slop aesthetic — and understand why the two tools need to be prompted differently to get there.
structure_plan: What makes an image look 'AI' in the first place (and what to do instead)
How to prompt ChatGPT for realistic images — with example prompts
How to prompt Midjourney for realistic images — with example prompts
claims_to_verify: ChatGPT image generation is powered by DALL-E 3 (or confirm if a newer model is now default)
Midjourney's most current default model version and whether --style raw or equivalent parameters still exist and function as described
Whether Midjourney still supports stylize, chaos, and ar parameters in current versions
Whether ChatGPT image gen accepts camera/lens/film-style language meaningfully or largely ignores it compared to Midjourney
Whether Midjourney's 'photo' or 'photorealistic' style modes have changed in recent model updates
Confirm that ChatGPT image generation is available to free and paid users, and whether any meaningful difference in quality or access exists between tiers
Confirm Midjourney's current access model (subscription required, no free tier as of recent changes)
Whether negative prompting syntax works in current Midjourney (--no parameter) and if ChatGPT image gen supports any equivalent
Specific prompt elements that are broadly agreed to push realism: e.g. specifying lens type, film grain, natural lighting, imperfections, specific time of day, shallow depth of field
brief.research_plan: Prioritize Midjourney's official documentation and changelog for current parameter syntax and model defaults. For ChatGPT image gen, use OpenAI's official feature pages and any current model notes. Supplement with well-regarded prompt engineering community sources (Midjourney subreddit, PromptHero, creator-focused YouTube channels) specifically for the 'what actually works' prompt language — but flag anything that comes only from community sources rather than official docs, since model updates can make community tips stale fast. Avoid relying on any single blog post for parameter specifics. Focus research on the *why* behind realism techniques (imperfection, specificity, photographic language) as much as the exact syntax, since that reasoning is more durable than any particular parameter value.

sources.title: Introducing 4o Image Generation
claims_supported: ChatGPT image generation is NO LONGER powered by DALL-E 3 — it was replaced by GPT-4o native image generation (also called '4o Image Generation'), which rolled out to Plus, Pro, Team, and Free users as the default image generator in ChatGPT
GPT-4o image generation excels at accurately rendering text, precisely following prompts, and leveraging 4o's inherent knowledge base and chat context, including uploaded images
DALL-E 3 is still accessible via a dedicated DALL-E GPT in the GPT store, but is no longer the default
The model is available to Free users (with limits) and all paid tiers

sources.title: Version – Midjourney (Official Documentation)
claims_supported: The current default Midjourney version is V8.1, which was released on midjourney.com on April 30, 2026, and became the default version on June 10, 2026
V6.1 was the default until June 16, 2025; V7 was default from June 17, 2025 until V8.1 replaced it
--style raw has a V8 equivalent: on V8 family write --raw (not --style raw); --style raw is still correct syntax when explicitly selecting V7
The --chaos parameter is confirmed active and documented for V8.1 (listed under Midjourney Controls official docs)

sources.title: Midjourney Controls – Official Documentation (Parameter List, Stylize, Chaos, No, Raw)
claims_supported: --chaos (or --c) parameter is confirmed active in current Midjourney, including V8.1
--stylize (or --s) parameter is confirmed active in current Midjourney, including V8.1 — described as controlling 'artistic flair'
--no parameter (negative prompt) is confirmed active in current Midjourney V8.1 — documented as 'Tell Midjourney what you don't want in your image'
--ar (aspect ratio) parameter is confirmed active and functional in current versions
Raw mode is confirmed active for V8.1 — documented as a separate control option (written as --raw for V8 family, --style raw for V7)

sources.title: Free Trials – Midjourney (Official Documentation)
claims_supported: No free trial is currently available in Discord or the midjourney.com website
A limited trial is available only on the niji·journey app (iOS and Android)
Midjourney requires a paid subscription to generate images on the main platform

sources.title: Midjourney 8.1 Guide: Prompts, Omni Reference & Parameters
sources.publisher: blakecrosley.com (independent review, well-sourced with official citations)
claims_supported: V8.1 is confirmed as the default model as of June 11, 2026; V7 remains fully selectable
--style raw is V7 syntax and still correct when V7 is explicitly selected; on V8 family write --raw
V7 strengths for realism: natural language understanding, excellent photorealism, strong text rendering, better human anatomy (hands, bodies), improved spatial relationships
Full V7 parameter surface confirmed: includes Omni Reference, Quality values, multi-prompts, No parameter (negative prompting)
V8.1-specific parameters confirmed: --hd (native 2048px output), --sd (standard definition), --raw (literal prompt control), --ar (aspect ratio), --chaos still present

sources.title: V7 is now the default model! (Official Midjourney Update Announcement)
claims_supported: V7 became the default Midjourney model on June 17, 2025 (now superseded by V8.1 as of June 2026)
V7 introduced Draft Mode (--draft, 10x speed), Omni-reference (--oref), and updated Style Reference (sref) algorithms
V7 described officially as 'a smarter model with higher image quality, better prompt understanding, and improved image coherence'

sources.title: Midjourney Pricing: Complete Guide to Plans 2026
sources.publisher: techjacksolutions.com (independent, fact-checked against vendor docs, verified June 9, 2026)
claims_supported: Midjourney removed its free trial in March 2023 and has been subscription-only since
There is no free plan and no free trial as of June 2026; the cheapest entry point is $10/month Basic plan
Midjourney has four monthly plans: Basic ($10), Standard ($30), Pro ($60), Mega ($120); annual billing gives 20% discount
Midjourney bills by fast GPU time, not by image count
Basic plan: 3.3 fast GPU hours/month; Standard: 15 hours; Pro: 30 hours; Mega: 60 hours
All plan tiers produce the same image quality — users pay for speed and privacy (Stealth Mode on Pro/Mega), not quality differences

sources.title: GPT Image Generation Models Prompting Guide (OpenAI Developer Cookbook)
claims_supported: OpenAI officially confirms that photography language (lens, lighting, framing) meaningfully steers realism in GPT image generation — this is not ignored

Officially recommended approach: 'prompt the model as if a real photo is being captured in the moment' — use photography language and explicitly ask for real texture (pores, wrinkles, fabric wear, imperfections)
Avoid words that imply studio polish or staging
Camera/lens specs may be interpreted loosely for exact physical simulation, but are useful for high-level look and composition
'Photorealistic', 'real photograph', 'taken on a real camera', 'professional photography', or 'iPhone photo' all help engage photorealistic mode
Prompt structure guidance: background/scene → subject → key details → constraints; include intended use to set level of polish
For photorealism, 'camera/composition terms (lens, aperture feel, lighting) often steer realism more reliably than generic 8K/ultra-detailed'
ChatGPT image generation does NOT support a formal negative prompt parameter like Midjourney's --no; negative guidance is written inline in natural language

sources.title: ChatGPT image generation: What's changed and why it matters
sources.publisher: Jisc National Centre for AI (independent education technology body)
claims_supported: GPT-4o shows noticeably improved photorealism over DALL-E 3; 'many of the usual giveaways, such as facial features and hands, have been significantly improved'
The level of photorealism in GPT-4o outputs was described as 'remarkable' in direct comparison to DALL-E 3 on identical prompts
Poses feel more natural, and overall image quality including lighting, shadows, clothing, and textures is noticeably enhanced in GPT-4o vs DALL-E 3
Free users experience rate limits and queue-based processing (cool-down periods) distinguishing their experience from paid tiers

sources.title: ChatGPT Image Generation Access Model: Free vs Paid Tiers (AI Tool Radar)
claims_supported: GPT-4o native image generation replaced DALL-E 3 in the ChatGPT interface in December 2025 (rolled out from March 2025, became full default by December 2025)
ChatGPT Free tier includes limited GPT-4o image generations with queue-based processing and dynamic usage caps
ChatGPT Plus ($20/mo) raises limits and adds priority processing, but exact image count per day is not publicly documented and varies with server load
ChatGPT Pro ($200/mo) offers the highest limits and fastest processing
OpenAI does not publish a fixed image quota for any tier
GPT-4o is NOT DALL-E 4 — it is a fundamentally different architecture (omnimodel) where image generation is built natively into the language model

unverifiable_claims: The claim that ChatGPT 'largely ignores' camera/lens/film-style language compared to Midjourney is NOT verified and should be softened or dropped. OpenAI's own official prompting guide explicitly endorses and recommends using photography language (lens, lighting, framing) for realism, and states it steers results meaningfully. The difference is that ChatGPT interprets camera specs loosely (for composition/look rather than exact physical simulation), and uses natural language rather than CLI-style syntax — but it does not ignore this language.

Whether Midjourney has a distinct 'photo' or 'photorealistic' style mode as a named toggle in current V8.1 could not be confirmed. V7 and V8.1 support --raw (reduces aesthetic interpretation, increases prompt literalism) and --style raw (V7 syntax), but a specific named 'photorealistic mode' toggle was not confirmed in current documentation. Writers should use 'raw mode' language rather than implying a dedicated 'photorealistic mode' switch.

Specific numeric stylize values best suited for realism in V8.1 (e.g., exact recommended --stylize range for photorealism) could not be verified from official V8.1 docs directly — the general principle (lower stylize = more literal/prompt-faithful, higher = more artistic) is verified, but specific recommended ranges for V8.1 realism are community/editorial observations, not official specs.

Whether the --no parameter in Midjourney V8.1 is reliably effective (vs. inconsistent/less predictable) is noted in independent reviews as variable — the parameter exists and is documented, but its reliability for complex exclusions is editorially described as imperfect. Writers should caveat this.

The claim that Midjourney is accessible for free via Meta AI (meta.ai) was reported by one pricing-focused independent source but could not be cross-verified from official Midjourney or Meta sources within this research pass. Writers should treat this as unverified and not cite it as a confirmed free access path.

article.title: How to Make AI Images Look Real, Not Obviously AI
alternative_titles: Stop Getting AI-Slop: A Prompting Guide for ChatGPT and Midjourney
Prompting for Realism: ChatGPT and Midjourney Tips That Actually Work
article.dek: Glossy skin, creepy hands, that plasticky sheen, here's how to prompt your way out of it on both ChatGPT and Midjourney.
article.meta_description: Learn how to write prompts for ChatGPT and Midjourney that produce realistic, lifelike images instead of obvious AI-slop. Includes ready-to-copy example prompts for both tools.
article.quick_answer: The biggest fix is specificity: describe your scene like a photographer setting up a shot, not like you're ordering a stock photo. Add imperfections, real lighting conditions, and camera language, and avoid anything that sounds like 'professional studio render.'
article.introduction: You type something reasonable, hit generate, and get back something that looks like a video game cutscene from 2019. Too smooth, too lit, too fake. The fix isn't a secret setting, it's how you write the prompt. ChatGPT and Midjourney both respond to the same core idea: describe a real moment, not an ideal one. But they need to hear it differently.

sections.heading: Why AI images look fake, and the one mindset shift that helps
sections.body_markdown:
AI image generators default to a kind of hyper-polished average. They've seen millions of stock photos, studio shoots, and CGI renders, so when you give them a vague prompt, that's the aesthetic they reach for.

The counter-move is to be specific in ways that imply *reality*, not quality. A real photo has a time of day, a slightly messy background, imperfect skin, a specific lens doing a specific thing. A fake-looking AI image has none of that, just vibes.

A few things that reliably push results toward real:

- **Name a specific light source.** "Overcast afternoon light" beats "good lighting" every time.
- **Add something imperfect.** Mention worn fabric, uneven skin texture, a shadow that falls at an odd angle.
- **Give it a context.** Where is this? What just happened? A subject in a specific place looks more real than a subject floating in a void.
- **Avoid words that imply polish.** "Professional," "studio," "ultra-detailed," and "8K" tend to pull the image toward render-land. Camera and photography terms tend to steer toward something more realistic, OpenAI's own documentation notes that framing, but the specifics are worth checking directly if you want the source.

sections.heading: Prompting ChatGPT for realistic images
sections.body_markdown:
ChatGPT's image generation now runs on GPT-4o's native image model, not DALL-E 3, and it's noticeably better at hands, faces, and natural lighting than its predecessor. There's a free tier to try it, though access is dynamic and OpenAI doesn't publish fixed quotas, so how much you can actually do for free on a given day varies. Paid plans give you more consistent headroom.

The key thing to know: ChatGPT understands natural language really well, so write your prompt like you're describing a scene to a director, not configuring a settings panel. Framing it as if a real photo is being *captured in the moment* is a useful mental model, and explicitly asking for real texture like pores, fabric wear, and wrinkles helps.

Camera and lens language works here. It won't simulate exact optics, but "shot on a 50mm lens, shallow depth of field" tends to shift the feel toward something photographic. Negative guidance goes inline, there's no `--no` parameter, so you just say what you don't want in the prompt itself.

Good structure for a realistic ChatGPT prompt: **setting → subject → key details → what you don't want.**

prompts.label: Candid portrait, natural light, imperfect skin
prompts.prompt: A candid photo of a woman in her 40s sitting at a small wooden table in a dimly lit café, late afternoon light coming through a window to her left. She's looking slightly off-camera. Natural skin texture, slight under-eye shadows, no makeup. Shot on a 50mm lens, shallow depth of field, slightly soft background. No studio lighting, no retouching, no perfect symmetry.

prompts.label: Outdoor street scene, documentary feel
prompts.prompt: A real photograph of a man in his 30s walking down a wet city street at dusk, motion slightly blurred, puddles reflecting neon signs. Worn jacket, slightly hunched against the wind. Shot from across the street, 85mm equivalent, grain visible. No posed look, no dramatic lighting setup, no stock-photo composition.

prompts.label: Interior room, lived-in and believable
prompts.prompt: A photo of a small, cluttered home office, desk covered in papers and a half-empty coffee mug, warm lamp light, window showing overcast sky outside. No one in the frame. Shot like a documentary still, 35mm lens feel, natural shadows, slightly imperfect white balance. No staged or minimalist aesthetic.

sections.heading: Prompting Midjourney for realistic images
sections.body_markdown:
Midjourney's current default model is V8.1, which became default in early June 2026 (the official docs and at least one independent source give slightly different exact dates, so double-check if it matters). It's subscription-only, no free tier on the main platform, so you'll need at least the Basic plan to use it.

Midjourney is more parameter-driven than ChatGPT, which means you have more dials to turn, but also more ways to accidentally turn the wrong one. For realism specifically, two parameters matter most:

- **`--raw`** tells V8.1 to interpret your prompt more literally, dialing back the artistic interpretation the model normally adds. This is your single biggest tool for avoiding that painterly, over-styled look.
- **`--stylize` (or `--s`)** controls how much aesthetic flair the model applies. Lower values mean it sticks closer to what you actually described. The default leans artistic, pulling it down nudges results toward documentary.

The **`--no` parameter** lets you explicitly exclude things (`--no soft focus, studio lighting, plastic skin`). It's documented and active in V8.1, though from what I've seen reported, it works better for straightforward exclusions than complex ones, so treat it as a nudge, not a guarantee.

Midjourney also responds well to photographic language, but unlike ChatGPT, it lets you lean harder into technical terms, film stock names, specific lens focal lengths, lighting setups, since it was trained heavily on photography-tagged data.

prompts.label: Candid portrait, raw mode, low stylize
prompts.prompt: candid photograph of a woman in her early 50s sitting outside a café, overcast morning light, natural skin texture, slight windblown hair, worn linen jacket, looking slightly past the camera, 85mm f/1.8, shallow depth of field, film grain, muted tones --raw --s 50 --ar 4:5 --no studio lighting, retouching, symmetrical composition

prompts.label: Urban street scene, documentary style
prompts.prompt: documentary street photography, man in his 30s crossing a busy intersection in the rain, motion blur on passing cars, neon reflections in wet pavement, natural ambient light, worn sneakers, looking down at his phone, shot from mid-distance, 35mm f/2, slight grain, slightly underexposed --raw --s 40 --ar 3:2 --no dramatic lighting, stock photo feel, posed expression

prompts.label: Environmental portrait, real imperfection
prompts.prompt: environmental portrait of an older man in his 70s in a cluttered workshop, dusty afternoon light from a single window, sawdust on his shirt, weathered hands holding a tool, background shelves with jars and wood scraps, slightly harsh shadows, shot on film, 50mm, candid moment --raw --s 60 --ar 4:3 --no soft focus, clean background, professional lighting

key_takeaways: Describe a real captured moment, not an ideal rendered one, specificity about light, place, and imperfection does most of the work
For ChatGPT, write naturally and include what you *don't* want inline; for Midjourney, use `--raw` and lower `--stylize` values to reduce the default artistic polish
Midjourney requires a paid subscription; ChatGPT image gen has a free tier, but access is dynamic and limits aren't publicly documented, so treat it as a way to try things out, not a reliable quota
article.conclusion:
The prompts above are starting points, not recipes, tweak the details to fit your actual scene. The underlying principle holds across both tools: the more your prompt sounds like a photographer describing a real shot, the less it'll look like a machine guessing what "good" means.

We didn't run controlled A/B tests for this guide, the prompt techniques here are drawn from OpenAI's official GPT-4o image generation announcement, Midjourney's official docs, and a few independent reviews and pricing guides. Model updates can shift what works, so if something stops landing, the Midjourney docs and OpenAI's developer resources are worth a direct check.
article.methodology_disclosure: This guide was researched using OpenAI's official GPT-4o image generation announcement and developer prompting cookbook, Midjourney's official documentation for V8.1 parameters and version history, an independent educational review from Jisc's National Centre for AI, and two independent pricing and feature guides. Claims about parameter syntax and model defaults were verified against primary official sources wherever possible. Community-sourced observations about parameter reliability (specifically --no behavior) are noted as editorial rather than official. No images were generated or tested by dailyblip during the production of this guide.

issues.original_text: Midjourney's current default model is V8.1, which became default in early June 2026
issues.issue: The article says V8.1 became default 'in early June 2026' but the primary source (Midjourney official docs) gives June 10, 2026 and the independent source gives June 11, 2026 — 'early June' is loosely accurate but the hedged disclaimer ('official docs and at least one independent source give slightly different exact dates') implies more conflict than exists between those two dates, and obscures that a verified date is available.

issues.original_text: from what I've seen reported, it works better for straightforward exclusions
issues.issue: The phrase 'from what I've seen reported' implies first-person editorial observation by the author/dailyblip, which contradicts the methodology disclosure stating no testing was done and creates undisclosed ambiguity about the source of this claim.

issues.original_text: OpenAI's own documentation notes that framing, but the specifics are worth checking directly
issues.issue: This vague attribution gesture neither cites the source properly nor gives the reader enough to verify it, despite a specific primary source (OpenAI developer cookbook) existing in the source list that directly supports the claim.

issues.original_text: There's a free tier to try it, though access is dynamic and OpenAI doesn't publish fixed quotas
issues.issue: The article frames this as 'a free tier to try it,' which undersells the limitation; the source indicates access is queue-based with dynamic caps, making it unreliable for practical use, and the key_takeaways section characterizes it more accurately than s2 does.

issues.original_text: ChatGPT image gen has a free tier, but access is dynamic and limits aren't publicly documented
issues.issue: This is more accurate than s2 but still somewhat inconsistent with the source, which specifies queue-based processing and dynamic usage caps — 'limits aren't publicly documented' is correct but omits the queue/cooldown mechanic that meaningfully affects usability.

issues.original_text: the prompt techniques here are drawn from OpenAI's official GPT-4o image generation announcement
issues.issue: The conclusion credits the OpenAI announcement as a prompting source, but the substantive prompting guidance actually comes from the OpenAI developer cookbook, not the product announcement — this misattributes the primary editorial basis.

issues.original_text: ChatGPT's image generation now runs on GPT-4o's native image model, not DALL-E 3
issues.issue: The independent source notes the transition was rolled out from March 2025 and became full default by December 2025, so 'now' is accurate but omitting this timeline context could mislead readers who used ChatGPT during the transition period and remember DALL-E 3 behavior.

issues.original_text: --stylize (or --s) controls how much aesthetic flair the model applies. The default leans artistic
issues.issue: The article states the default 'leans artistic' without specifying the actual default --stylize value, which matters practically; Midjourney's official docs document a specific default value that would be useful to readers adjusting this parameter.

warnings: s3: The article says V8.1 became default 'in early June 2026' but the primary source (Midjourney official docs) gives June 10, 2026 and the independent source gives June 11, 2026 — 'early June' is loosely accurate but the hedged disclaimer ('official docs and at least one independent source give slightly different exact dates') implies more conflict than exists between those two dates, and obscures that a verified date is available.

s3: The phrase 'from what I've seen reported' implies first-person editorial observation by the author/dailyblip, which contradicts the methodology disclosure stating no testing was done and creates undisclosed ambiguity about the source of this claim.

images.prompt: Flat vector illustration, 16:9, deep navy gradient background (#071A1F to #0C242B). Centered composition. On the center-left, a large geometric amber (#FFB454) camera viewfinder frame — clean rectangular outline with corner marks. Inside the viewfinder, a small flat scene icon: a simplified human figure standing in warm, slightly directional overcast light, with a subtle imperfect shadow falling to one side, and a hint of a real background (faint horizon line, slightly uneven). On the right side, a semi-transparent, faded flat icon representing a generic stock-photo output — perfectly smooth, symmetrical, lifeless, rendered in muted aqua-grey (#63D8C6 at low opacity). A soft ambient glow of amber light around the viewfinder element. The two elements are horizontally balanced but visually weighted toward the amber/real left side. All elements stay at least 10% margin from every frame edge — nothing cropped, nothing touching the border. No text anywhere in the image. Polished infographic style, crisp geometric shapes, flat color fills, no texture, no photorealism, no painterly rendering, no floating UI panels. Should read clearly as a thumbnail.
images.alt_text: A camera viewfinder in amber frames a specific, imperfectly lit scene, contrasted against a faded, over-smooth generic icon — illustrating the gap between a real-looking AI image and a fake-looking one.
images.caption: The difference between a fake-looking AI image and a believable one usually comes down to specificity: real light, real imperfection, real framing intent.
images.concept: A photographer's eye — framing a shot with specificity: named light source, imperfect detail, real place — contrasted against a flat, over-polished AI default output.
images.purpose: Immediately communicate the article's core premise: the gap between a vague prompt that produces a fake-looking render and a specific, grounded prompt that produces something believably real.

images.prompt: Flat vector illustration, 1:1 square canvas, deep navy background (#0C242B). Canvas split vertically down the center by a thin aqua (#63D8C6) dividing line. LEFT HALF: a simplified flat icon of a generic, perfectly symmetric, smooth human figure or object — muted grey-amber color, perfectly centered in its half, overly smooth and static. Small short amber text label 'vague' in a clean sans-serif near the top of the left panel, well inside the safe margin. RIGHT HALF: the same type of icon but with clear specificity — a small amber sun icon positioned upper-right casting a visible directional shadow to the lower-left of the figure, the subject slightly off-center, a tiny imperfection marker (small notch or irregular edge) on the outline. Small aqua text label 'specific' near the top of the right panel. Both icons and all labels are at minimum 12% margin from every edge of the canvas — nothing touching the border. The right half reads warmer and more alive. Flat color fills, no texture, no photorealism, crisp geometric shapes, polished infographic style. No floating UI, no AI brain imagery.
images.alt_text: Two-panel comparison: a vague prompt produces a smooth, generic, lifeless icon on the left; a specific prompt with named directional light and a slight imperfection produces a warmer, more grounded result on the right.
images.caption: Specificity does most of the work. Naming a light source, a time of day, and one real imperfection moves the result from stock-photo aesthetic to something that feels captured.
images.concept: A direct visual of the 'mindset shift' — two prompts, two visual outcomes: vague/stock-photo aesthetic on one side vs. specific/real-captured-moment on the other.
images.purpose: Show readers exactly what 'specificity' means in practice — the left prompt is vague and produces a polished-average result; the right prompt names a light source, a moment, an imperfection, and produces something grounded.

images.prompt: Flat vector illustration, 16:9 landscape canvas, deep navy background (#071A1F). Three horizontally arranged rounded-rectangle tiles centered vertically, connected by amber (#FFB454) rightward-pointing arrows between them. Generous margins: all three tiles are centered in the canvas with at least 12% clear space on the outer left edge before tile 1 and on the outer right edge after tile 3 — nothing touches the frame border. TILE 1 (leftmost): dark navy fill, a flat amber clapperboard icon centered in the tile, amber numeral '1' above the tile. TILE 2 (center): dark navy fill, a flat aqua (#63D8C6) combined sun-and-cloud icon representing overcast light, amber numeral '2' above. TILE 3 (rightmost): dark navy fill, a flat icon of a generic smooth rectangle with a bold amber X or strikethrough across it, representing 'what you don't want,' amber numeral '3' above. Amber arrow icons between tile 1→2 and tile 2→3, horizontally aligned. Even spacing between all three tiles. No text labels other than the three numerals. Flat color fills, no texture, no photorealism, no floating UI panels, crisp geometric shapes, polished infographic style. All elements stay within a safe margin from every edge of the frame.
images.alt_text: A three-step workflow for ChatGPT image prompts: step 1, describe the scene like a director; step 2, name the specific light source; step 3, include what you don't want inline.
images.caption: ChatGPT understands natural language well — so write your prompt like you're briefing a director: scene, light, and inline negatives all in one go.
images.concept: A prompt-building workflow for ChatGPT image generation: three sequential steps — describe the scene like a director, name the specific light, add what you don't want inline — flowing left to right.
images.purpose: Walk readers through the ChatGPT-specific prompting approach described in the article: natural language, director framing, inline negative intent — as a clear three-step sequence.

images.prompt: Flat vector illustration, 9:16 portrait canvas, deep navy background (#0C242B). Canvas split horizontally into two equal halves by a thin aqua (#63D8C6) dividing line across the full width, centered vertically. TOP HALF: dark navy panel. A simplified flat icon of a generic subject (humanoid silhouette or simple object) centered in the panel — overly smooth, symmetric, with a soft radiant amber glow emanating from behind it suggesting over-stylized polish. Short amber (#FFB454) text label 'default' placed near the top of the top panel, well within a 10% safe margin from the top edge. BOTTOM HALF: same dark navy panel. The same type of icon but rendered flatter and more literal — slightly off-center, with a small directional aqua (#63D8C6) light source indicator at upper left casting a subtle shadow, and a minor imperfection on the outline (small notch). Short aqua text label '--raw' placed near the bottom of the bottom panel, well within a 10% safe margin from the bottom edge. All icons and labels have at least 10% clear margin from every edge of the canvas — nothing cropped. Even spacing, flat fills, no texture, no photorealism, polished infographic style. Two text labels total. No Midjourney logo.
images.alt_text: Vertical comparison: the default Midjourney output (top) shows an over-stylized, glowing, artificially smooth result; the --raw version (bottom) is flatter, more literal, with directional light and a subtle imperfection.
images.caption: Midjourney's --raw flag tells V8.1 to interpret your prompt more literally, dialing back its default artistic polish. Pair it with a lower --stylize value for the most grounded results.
images.concept: A vertical stacked comparison of two Midjourney parameter states: high --stylize (default, over-polished, artistic) vs. low --stylize with --raw (literal, grounded, real-looking).
images.purpose: Visualize exactly what the article says about Midjourney's two key realism parameters — --raw and lower --stylize values — and what they change about the output.

images.prompt: Flat vector illustration, 1:1 square canvas, deep navy background (#071A1F). A 2×2 grid of four equal rounded-rectangle tiles, centered in the canvas with at least 12% clear margin on all four outer edges (top, bottom, left, right) — no tile touches or approaches the frame border. Even gutter spacing between tiles. TILE 1 (top-left): amber (#FFB454) fill, centered flat icon of a combined sun and cloud representing named overcast light. TILE 2 (top-right): aqua (#63D8C6) fill, centered flat icon of a simple object outline with a small notch or irregular edge representing imperfection. TILE 3 (bottom-left): amber (#FFB454) fill, centered flat icon of a camera aperture (geometric circle with petal blades) representing camera language. TILE 4 (bottom-right): aqua (#63D8C6) fill, centered flat icon of a smooth rectangle with a bold X strikethrough representing what you don't want. All four icons are the same size and weight. No text anywhere in the image. Flat color fills, no texture, no photorealism, no floating UI, crisp geometric shapes, polished infographic style. Alternating amber/aqua tile fills create a visual rhythm.
images.alt_text: A 2×2 icon grid showing the four specificity ingredients for realistic AI images: a named light source (sun and cloud), an imperfection marker, camera language (aperture), and an inline negative (strikethrough).
images.caption: Four things that reliably move AI image results toward real: a named light source, one real imperfection, camera-language framing, and telling the model what you don't want.
images.concept: A visual checklist of the four 'specificity ingredients' that reliably push AI images toward real — named light source, imperfect detail, camera language, and inline negative — each as a distinct icon in a 2×2 grid.
images.purpose: Give readers a scannable visual summary of the article's most actionable tactics from the key takeaways — the specific ingredients to add to any prompt.

images.prompt: Flat vector illustration, 16:9 landscape canvas, deep navy background (#0C242B). Canvas split vertically by a thin amber (#FFB454) center dividing line running the full height. LEFT PANEL (left half of canvas): dark navy fill. Centered in the panel: a large flat amber (#FFB454) speech bubble icon (representing natural language input). Below it, a smaller aqua (#63D8C6) padlock-open icon (representing free tier access). Short amber text label 'ChatGPT' near the top of the left panel, well within safe margins. RIGHT PANEL (right half of canvas): dark navy fill. Centered in the panel: a large flat aqua (#63D8C6) dial or horizontal slider icon (representing parameter-driven input). Below it, a smaller amber (#FFB454) key icon (representing subscription requirement). Short aqua text label 'Midjourney' near the top of the right panel, well within safe margins. ALL elements — icons, labels, and the divider endpoints — stay at least 12% clear margin from every outer edge of the canvas (left edge, right edge, top edge, bottom edge). Two text labels total. Flat color fills, no texture, no photorealism, no floating UI panels, crisp geometric shapes, polished infographic style. The left panel reads warm/amber; the right reads cool/aqua.
images.alt_text: Side-by-side comparison of ChatGPT image generation (natural language, speech bubble, free tier padlock) and Midjourney (parameter-driven, dial icon, subscription key) — the two tools covered in this guide.
images.caption: ChatGPT understands natural language and has a free tier to experiment with; Midjourney is more parameter-driven and requires a paid subscription. Both reward specificity, but you talk to them differently.
images.concept: A side-by-side tool comparison: ChatGPT image gen (natural language, inline negatives, free tier exists) vs. Midjourney (parameter-driven, --raw flag, subscription required) — visualized through their distinct interaction metaphors, not logos.
images.purpose: Help readers quickly grasp the practical difference between the two tools covered in this article — how you talk to each one and what the access situation looks like — so they can choose the right starting point.

submitted.idea: 10 Prompts to make your Suno music better

brief.target_reader: Someone who has tried Suno once or twice and gotten okay results, but isn't sure how to write prompts that actually sound like what they're imagining
brief.reader_outcome: Feel confident enough to go back to Suno and write a noticeably better prompt today
structure_plan: Why your Suno prompts probably aren't working yet
10 prompts (and what makes each one tick)
claims_to_verify: Suno uses a text prompt (style tags, mood, instrumentation, etc.) to guide music generation — confirm what the prompt input field actually accepts and how it's labeled in the current UI
Suno supports a 'custom mode' or similar that lets users write lyrics and style descriptions separately — confirm this feature exists and what it's called
Suno responds to genre, mood, tempo feel (e.g. 'slow,' 'driving'), instrumentation, and vocal style descriptors — confirm which of these actually influence output reliably vs. inconsistently
Suno does NOT reliably respond to BPM numbers or music-theory-specific terms — confirm whether this is still the case or whether it has improved
Whether Suno's prompt field has a character limit that affects how detailed a prompt can be
Whether Suno allows negative prompting (telling it what NOT to include) — confirm if this is supported
brief.research_plan: Prioritize Suno's own documentation and official help/FAQ pages for UI details and supported prompt features. Supplement with hands-on creator writeups, Reddit threads (r/SunoAI), and YouTube tutorials from active users to understand what prompt patterns actually work in practice. Avoid relying on single blog posts for capability claims — cross-check anything about what Suno 'understands' against multiple user reports. Since this is a prompting guide not a pricing guide, skip plan/quota details entirely.

sources.title: How do I exclude elements of a song?
claims_supported: Suno officially supports an 'Exclude' mechanism for negative prompting — users can specify genres, specific instruments, voice styles, and more that they do NOT want in the output
The feature is called 'Exclude' in Suno's own documentation

sources.title: Create in V4.5: Better Prompts in Lyrics
claims_supported: Suno has a Style field for genre-specific information and a separate Lyrics box — these are distinct prompt inputs in the current UI
Custom Mode exists as a named feature in the current Suno UI
Creative Sliders (Weirdness) are available in Custom Mode in V4.5+
V4.5 was the model version that introduced improved prompt handling in the Lyrics box

sources.title: Suno on X: 'You can now Exclude Styles when making new songs'
claims_supported: Suno officially announced an 'Exclude Styles' feature accessible in Custom Mode for Pro and Premier subscribers
Users can exclude specific instruments, styles, or vocal styles (e.g. male/female vocals)
Excluded styles appear in the Song Preview with a '-' prefix (e.g., -piano)
The feature is accessed via Create > Custom > 'Exclude Styles' switch (Pro/Premier only)

sources.title: Suno AI Prompt & Lyrics Character Limits: The Complete Cheat Sheet (2026)
claims_supported: On current Suno models (V4.5, V4.5+, V5, V5.5), custom lyrics cap at 5,000 characters and the style prompt caps at 1,000 characters
On older models (V3.5, V4), those caps drop to 3,000 (lyrics) and 200 (style)
A dedicated 'negative_tags' API field exists for styles to avoid, with a 200-character limit across all model versions
Suno does not publish an official limits table; app-side numbers can shift between UI releases
Simple Mode song description (gpt_description_prompt) is limited to 400 characters on every model version

sources.title: Suno Character Limits (2026): 5,000 Lyrics on V4.5+ — Every Field by Model
claims_supported: V4.5/V5/V5.5 style field = 1,000 chars; lyrics = 5,000 chars; title = 100 chars; V4 and older = 200/3,000/80
Past ~3,000 characters in the lyrics field (~60 lines), Suno tends to rush, skip sections, or shorten output — ~3,000 is described as the quality sweet spot even when the box allows more
A 'Negative Style' or 'Exclude' field exists where available, accepting entries like 'no vocals,' 'no drums,' 'no autotune'
Negative phrases like 'no percussion' in the main style prompt sometimes work but are less reliable than using the dedicated Exclude field
Suno silently truncates content that runs past field caps with no warning to the user

sources.title: Suno AI Tutorial: Turn Text Prompts Into Full Songs (Simple, Instrumental, Custom)
claims_supported: Custom Mode is a named feature in the current Suno UI; it lets users outline song parts (verse, bridge, chorus), supply their own lyrics or let Suno write them, and define mood, instrumentation, and vocal style
Simple Mode takes a plain English description and Suno handles lyrics, melody, instruments, and structure — no chord progressions or BPM settings required
Users can exclude sounds or genres within Custom Mode to avoid clashes (e.g., 'exclude classical elements and traditional orchestral hits')
Negative-style direction in Custom Mode is described as working for brand/picture constraints

sources.title: Custom style prompts for Suno
claims_supported: Custom Mode splits input into two panels: a Lyrics Generator (or custom lyrics box) and a Style of Music prompt
The Style of Music field encompasses mood, sub-genre descriptions, instruments, and vocal tags
The Lyrics box accommodates 3,000 characters (note: this may reflect an older model version — see character limit sources for V4.5+ update to 5,000)
Suno uses an LLM to convert the Style Prompt into music; words can be linked to time periods, cultures, specific song patterns, and emotions
Musical techniques like 'Polyrhythmic percussion, modal jazz harmonies, with a climactic key change' can be included in style prompts

sources.title: Why Suno Ignores Your Prompt (And How to Fix It)
claims_supported: Suno's Advanced Options include a dedicated Exclude field that functions significantly more reliably for negative prompting than writing negations in the Style prompt
Genre and mood associations dominate tempo — BPM output may not match a hard number written in the prompt; a BPM range is recommended over a hard number
Overuse of generic emotion words ('happy,' 'sad,' 'relaxing') maps to the broadest possible distribution of outputs and produces generic results

sources.title: Negative Prompting in Suno v5: Complete Guide
claims_supported: No official Suno 'negative prompt field' was found that guarantees absolute removal — negative prompting is a prompt-control method that is useful and repeatable but not absolute
Negative prompts are best treated as steering instructions, not hard bans — they reduce unwanted elements but do not guarantee their complete exclusion
1–2 clear exclusions work better than a long blacklist; replacing a removed element with a positive instruction improves results

sources.title: Suno AI Prompt Guide 2026: Copy-Paste Templates + The Formula That Actually Works
claims_supported: Negative prompts ('no autotune,' 'no electric guitar,' 'no drums') in the style field work better in v5.5 than in older versions — implying BPM/negative prompt reliability has improved with newer models

BPM numbers in prompts: 'Suno ignored BPM' is a known failure mode when tempo is described qualitatively; the fix recommended is to use a number (e.g., '128 BPM' not 'fast-paced') — suggesting BPM numbers do have some effect but are not guaranteed
Suno weighs earlier tags in the style field more heavily — genre should be placed first
65–80 BPM for relaxed focus works consistently in lo-fi instrumental prompts (recurring user/tester observation)

unverifiable_claims: The claim that Suno does NOT reliably respond to BPM numbers is contested and likely outdated: multiple independent sources from 2025–2026 recommend using specific BPM numbers and report improved BPM responsiveness in V4.5+/V5/V5.5. The writer should soften any blanket statement that BPM numbers are ignored, and instead note that BPM reliability has improved but is not guaranteed — genre/mood associations can override numeric tempo input.

The claim that Suno does not reliably respond to music-theory-specific terms is partially contradicted: independent sources report that specific music-theory language (e.g., 'modal jazz harmonies,' 'polyrhythmic percussion,' named ragas) does influence output, though results vary. The writer should frame this as 'inconsistent' rather than 'does not respond.'

Any specific labeling of the prompt input field in the current UI beyond 'Style of Music' and 'Lyrics' — Suno's official help pages retrieved do not provide a complete UI field-name glossary; field names may have changed across V4.5/V5/V5.5 releases.

Whether the 'Exclude Styles' feature (negative prompting via dedicated field) is available to free-tier users cannot be confirmed from sources retrieved — the official Suno announcement states it is available to 'Pro and Premier' subscribers only; free-tier availability is unverified.

The exact current character limit shown in the Suno app UI (as opposed to via API) is inconsistently reported across independent sources (some say 200, some 1,000 for the style field on V4/V5) — Suno does not publish an official limits table per the API source, so the writer should cite these as third-party-tested figures, not official specs.

article.title: 10 Prompts to Make Your Suno Music Actually Sound Like What You're Imagining
alternative_titles: Why Your Suno Prompts Aren't Working (and 10 That Do)
Better Suno Prompts: 10 Copy-Paste Examples That Actually Work
article.dek: Most people write Suno prompts the way they'd describe a song to a friend. That's the problem. Here's how to write them the way Suno actually listens.
article.meta_description: Struggling to get Suno to generate the music you're imagining? Here are 10 ready-to-use prompts — plus what makes each one work — so you can get noticeably better results today.
article.quick_answer: Suno responds best to specific genre, mood, instrumentation, and vocal style tags stacked together in the Style field. Vague emotional words like 'happy' or 'sad' tend to produce generic results — the more concrete and layered your description, the closer you'll get to what you're hearing in your head.
article.introduction: You typed something like "upbeat pop song about summer" and got... fine. Totally listenable, completely forgettable. The issue isn't Suno, it's that the prompt didn't give it much to work with. Suno has two separate input fields — a **Style** field and a **Lyrics** box — and what you put in each one matters differently. Once you understand that, prompts get a lot more useful.

sections.heading: Why vague prompts produce vague music
sections.body_markdown:
Suno's Style field is where the real steering happens. Genre, mood, tempo feel, instrumentation, vocal style — that's the language it responds to. Words like "happy" or "relaxing" are so broad that Suno has to guess, and it usually guesses toward the middle of everything.

A few things worth knowing before you start:

- **Put genre first.** Based on community testing (not official Suno docs), earlier tags in the Style field seem to carry more weight — so lead with genre and layer specifics after it. Worth trying, and widely reported, but no official confirmation.
- **BPM numbers can help, but aren't guaranteed.** Sources suggest BPM responsiveness has improved notably in V5.5 specifically, but the genre and mood you pick can still override a hard number regardless of version. Think of BPM as a nudge, not a command.
- **Music theory terms are hit-or-miss.** Phrases like "modal jazz harmonies" or "polyrhythmic percussion" do influence output, but results vary. Worth trying — just don't be surprised if it doesn't land precisely.
- **Negative prompting exists.** If you're on a Pro or Premier plan, Suno has a dedicated Exclude field (in Custom Mode) where you can list what you *don't* want — specific instruments, vocal styles, genres. Heads up: negative prompting in Suno isn't absolute, so treat it as a way to nudge things, not a hard filter. The exact input format may also vary depending on your model version, so check Suno's current help docs for syntax details. Putting negations in the main Style field is less reliable, though some users report it working in newer versions.
- **Don't go overboard on length.** The Style field has a character cap that varies by model version — roughly 1,000 characters on V4.5 and newer (per third-party testing), but older models like V3.5 and V4 cap out much lower, around 200 characters. If you're not sure which version you're on, keep your prompt tight just in case. A handful of well-chosen descriptors tends to work better than an exhaustive list anyway.

sections.heading: 10 prompts worth copying right now
sections.body_markdown: These go in Suno's **Style** field unless noted. Each one is built to be specific enough to actually steer the output somewhere interesting.

prompts.label: Moody lo-fi study beat
prompts.prompt: lo-fi hip hop, melancholic, jazzy chords, muffled drums, vinyl crackle, no vocals, 70 BPM, late night bedroom feel

prompts.label: Cinematic trailer build
prompts.prompt: orchestral, cinematic, building tension, strings and brass, epic percussion, no lyrics, slow to explosive, Hans Zimmer-inspired

prompts.label: Dark 80s synthwave
prompts.prompt: synthwave, dark, driving, pulsing bassline, analog synths, gated reverb drums, male vocals, cold and neon-lit, 110 BPM

prompts.label: Indie folk with an ache to it
prompts.prompt: indie folk, bittersweet, fingerpicked acoustic guitar, soft male vocals, sparse arrangement, slight reverb, storytelling, intimate

prompts.label: Upbeat funk for a short-form video
prompts.prompt: funk, upbeat, punchy brass, slap bass, tight drums, female vocals, playful and confident, radio-ready, 105 BPM

prompts.label: Ambient meditation track
prompts.prompt: ambient, meditative, drone pads, gentle piano, no drums, no vocals, spacious reverb, slow evolving textures, calming

prompts.label: Trap-soul R&B
prompts.prompt: R&B, trap-soul, silky female vocals, 808 bass, atmospheric synths, slow and sensual, AutoTune harmonies, late night vibes, 65 BPM

prompts.label: Acoustic pop with a hook
prompts.prompt: acoustic pop, warm, singalong chorus, strummed guitar, light percussion, female vocals, emotionally earnest, radio-friendly structure

prompts.label: Aggressive metal riff-based
prompts.prompt: heavy metal, aggressive, drop-tuned guitar riffs, double bass drums, shouted male vocals, raw energy, no synths, fast and relentless

prompts.label: Bossa nova café instrumental
prompts.prompt: bossa nova, relaxed, nylon-string guitar, brushed drums, upright bass, no vocals, sunny afternoon feel, Brazilian jazz influence

key_takeaways: Lead the Style field with genre, then layer mood, instruments, and vocal style after it — community testing suggests earlier tags carry more weight, though Suno hasn't confirmed this officially
BPM numbers nudge tempo but don't override genre — treat them as a hint, not a setting, and note that responsiveness varies by model version

The dedicated Exclude field (Pro/Premier only) is generally more targeted than writing negations in the Style prompt, but negative prompting in Suno isn't absolute — it reduces the chance of something showing up, not guarantees it
article.conclusion: None of these are magic — Suno still generates two or three variations and you pick the one that clicks. But specific prompts give you something to actually iterate on. Change one tag, regenerate, see what shifts. That's where the real control starts to feel like yours.
article.methodology_disclosure: This guide was researched using Suno's official help documentation and knowledge base, the official Suno X/Twitter account for feature announcements, and a range of independent creator write-ups, tutorials, and community-tested prompt guides from 2025–2026. Where claims about Suno's behavior (like BPM responsiveness or music-theory term handling) were contested or inconsistently reported across sources, the guide reflects that uncertainty rather than stating a clean answer. Prompts were constructed based on patterns consistently recommended across multiple independent sources, not tested directly by dailyblip. Field character limits cited are from third-party testing, since Suno does not publish an official limits table.

issues.original_text: roughly 1,000 characters on V4.5 and newer (per third-party testing)
issues.issue: The article conflates V4.5, V5, and V5.5 under one 1,000-character cap, but the source (aimusicapi.ai) lists V4.5, V4.5+, V5, and V5.5 separately — the article should not imply V5 and V5.5 share the V4.5 limit without noting V5/V5.5 may differ, and 'V4.5 and newer' overgeneralizes across model versions not confirmed to be identical.

issues.original_text: If you're on a Pro or Premier plan, Suno has a dedicated Exclude field
issues.issue: The official Suno X announcement source confirms the Exclude feature is Pro/Premier only in Custom Mode, but the article does not clarify that Basic/free-plan users lack access to this field entirely — a material omission for readers on the free tier.

issues.original_text: Sources suggest BPM responsiveness has improved notably in V5.5 specifically
issues.issue: No source specifically singles out V5.5 for BPM responsiveness improvement — the Roo/beehiiv source mentions v5.5 improved negative-prompt reliability in the style field, not BPM responsiveness; attributing the BPM claim to V5.5 specifically is unsupported.

issues.original_text: The dedicated Exclude field (Pro/Premier only) is generally more targeted than writing negations
issues.issue: The key takeaway presents the Exclude field as a Pro/Premier feature without noting it is unavailable on the free/Basic plan, which could mislead free-tier users into thinking they simply need to find the field rather than upgrade.

issues.original_text: The exact input format may also vary depending on your model version, so check Suno's current help docs for syntax details
issues.issue: The official Suno announcement shows the format is a '-' prefix (e.g., -piano) in the Song Preview — directing readers to 'check help docs for syntax' without noting the documented format undersells the available sourced information and may create unnecessary friction.

Referencing a living named artist in a prompt example could implicate copyright or style-cloning concerns under Suno's terms; the article does not flag any caution about using named artist references in prompts, which may conflict with Suno's content policies.

issues.original_text: older models like V3.5 and V4 cap out much lower, around 200 characters
issues.issue: The third-party source (aimusicapi.ai) lists V4 style cap as 200 characters but V3.5 is not explicitly listed separately — grouping V3.5 with V4 at 200 characters is an extrapolation not directly confirmed by the cited source.

issues.original_text: Suno has two separate input fields — a Style field and a Lyrics box
issues.issue: This characterization applies specifically to Custom Mode; in Simple Mode, Suno presents a single description field — the intro implies this two-field structure is the default UI experience for all users, which is inaccurate.

warnings: s1: The article conflates V4.5, V5, and V5.5 under one 1,000-character cap, but the source (aimusicapi.ai) lists V4.5, V4.5+, V5, and V5.5 separately — the article should not imply V5 and V5.5 share the V4.5 limit without noting V5/V5.5 may differ, and 'V4.5 and newer' overgeneralizes across model versions not confirmed to be identical.

s1: The official Suno X announcement source confirms the Exclude feature is Pro/Premier only in Custom Mode, but the article does not clarify that Basic/free-plan users lack access to this field entirely — a material omission for readers on the free tier.
